subject: English
chapter_title: The Day the River Spoke (Learning Together)
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 5 periods = 200 minutes
Total: 5 periods · 3h 20min

periods.activity_title: Before We Read: Sounds, Dreams, and Jahnavi

tasks_in_class.task_brief: After Section I: Jahnavi's dream and the River

phases.description: Teacher opens with the pre-reading activity: students close their eyes for a minute and listen to the sounds around them, then share with classmates and the teacher whether the sounds seem to carry a message (page 1). Pair-sharing is encouraged before whole-class discussion.

Teacher introduces Jahnavi — a bright girl in a coastal village — and poses the open prediction task: what could her dream be, and how might she make it come true (page 1)? Students share responses before the teacher begins reading Section I of the prose aloud.

Students read Section I independently, then discuss the two questions on Jahnavi's dream and whether the River can help her fulfil it (page 3). Teacher draws out the River's role as a gentle, personified counsellor.
Teacher consolidates by asking students to hold one 'wondering question' Jahnavi might ask the River, bridging to Section II in the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: This period sets the emotional core of the story — Jahnavi's exclusion from school and her longing to learn. Keep pre-reading oral activities brisk (8 minutes each) so Section I reading and discussion receive adequate time. Note that the detailed nature descriptions at the story's opening (kingfisher, lizard, river murmur) are worth a brief mention as the writer's way of immersing the reader in Jahnavi's world.

periods.activity_title: Jahnavi and the River: Questions, Advice, and Courage

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Table: Jahnavi's questions and River's answers
Extract 1 analysis: River's tone and Jahnavi's character

homework.task_brief: Let us think and reflect (pp.7–8): Extract 2 questions on River's advice

phases.description: Brief recap of Period 1: teacher asks two students to recall Jahnavi's main worry and the River's first response. This transitions the class into Section II of the prose.

Students read Section II and then complete the matching table — Jahnavi's curious questions in Column 1 and the River's answers in Column 2 (page 6). Teacher circulates and checks pairs before class-wide correction.

Class reads the extract 'You shouldn't cry, you know…I've got to hurry to reach the sea, you know' and answers the four questions on the River's tone, Jahnavi's repeated visits, the exclamation mark's effect, and the River's qualities (page 7). Teacher facilitates brief responses.
Teacher sets homework: complete the four questions on Extract 2 ('Can I do something?…They'd chase me out') at home (pages 7–8) and previews that the next period will examine Jahnavi's triumph.

periods.teacher_notes: The table task anchors students in the River's wit — especially the contrast between Jahnavi's wonder-questions about nature and the River's practical advice about going to school. In Extract 1, guide students to notice that the River is given a capital letter throughout the story, hinting at the personification concept that surfaces in the Vocabulary/Grammar spine. Extract 2 questions are moved to homework as time does not permit a third extract in this period.

periods.activity_title: Voices of School Life: Listening and Giving Advice

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Circle words used by five school-life speakers
Pair role-play: ask for and give advice, four situations

phases.description: Teacher previews the listening task: students read the ten word-box items aloud together so pronunciation is clear before audio begins (page 12, transcript p.39).

First listening: students circle words used by the five speakers from the word-box (two words are unused). Teacher plays/reads the transcript (p.39) at natural pace, then checks answers with the class.
Second listening: students match statements 1–7 to speakers (i)–(v); two statements are unused (page 12). After listening, pairs compare answers before class-wide correction.

Speaking task: pairs take turns asking for and giving advice across situations (i)–(iv) on page 13, modelled on the Jahnavi–River dialogue. Teacher monitors and gives brief oral feedback to two or three pairs.

periods.teacher_notes: Run both listening plays in succession without lengthy pauses to maintain focus; the gap between them should only be long enough for students to read the matching task. For the speaking activity, Situation (i) — joining an all-boys football team — echoes Jahnavi's gender-equity theme and often sparks the richest discussion; allow it a minute or two more if time permits. Remind students that listening and speaking activities should happen together as per NCF guidance.

periods.activity_title: Sound Words, Prepositions, and the River's Capital

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Personification: why 'River' has a capital letter
Fill blanks with prepositions in Jahnavi's school day
Distinguish preposition vs adverb in eight sentences

phases.description: Teacher leads Task I: why does the author capitalise 'River' when it is a common noun? Students discuss the clue (River given human qualities) and record a one-sentence answer (page 8). Teacher confirms the personification device.

Task II: students pick sound words from the text and match them to nine definitions (page 8), then fill the six blanks in the classroom scene using the matched sound words (page 9). Teacher corrects the matching on the board before students attempt the fill-in.

Task VI: students fill six blanks with prepositions from the brackets as Jahnavi walks through her first school day (page 11). Teacher explains each choice in context — 'at school' (location), 'outside the gate' (position), 'through the classroom' (movement direction).

Task VII: teacher explains that words like on, off, up, down can serve as either preposition or adverb depending on whether they link to a noun. Students complete eight sentences (pages 11–12) and two volunteers share answers for class discussion.
Quick consolidation: teacher asks students to give one example of a sound word and one of a preposition used in the day's tasks, then previews the writing period.

periods.teacher_notes: Task I links naturally to the extract analysis from Period 2 — students already noticed the River speaking — so the personification explanation should take no more than ten minutes. Tasks III (borrowed words) and IV–V (boats, crossword) are rich but time-consuming and are best left as self-study or home exploration; flag them briefly as optional extension. The preposition versus adverb distinction in Task VII is the hardest concept — use board examples before releasing students to work independently.

periods.activity_title: Observing Nature in Words; Exploring India's Rivers

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Draft descriptive paragraph on observed nature
Analyse poster: girls' dignity and national happiness

homework.task_brief: Let us write (p.14): Complete and refine nature observation paragraph at home

phases.description: Teacher introduces the writing task: over recent days students have been asked to observe colours, shapes, textures and movements in nature (page 14). Teacher models two or three sentence starters from the prompts table ('Every day, I see… / I find it very interesting that…') on the board.

Students draft their descriptive paragraph in class using the prompt table as a scaffold. Teacher circulates, offering feedback on vivid sensory detail and logical flow. Students also give a working title to their paragraph.

Teacher displays the poster 'The Happiness of a Nation lies in the Dignity of its Daughters' (page 15) and conducts a brief whole-class discussion on the poster's visual elements, why the girls look happy, and the relationship between dignity and happiness. Two or three students share responses.

Homework: students finish and refine their nature paragraph at home, aiming for at least five sentences using three different prompt starters. Teacher notes that Beyond-text tasks I (sacred rivers) and IV (government schemes) are available for self-study.

periods.teacher_notes: The writing task connects directly to the story's opening nature description — kingfisher, lizard, rustling bamboo — so encourage students to draw on those images as inspiration alongside their own observations. The poster discussion is kept brief (12 minutes) because it introduces the key theme of gender equity that runs across the chapter without requiring extended writing time in this period. Beyond-text tasks I and IV (sacred rivers discussion and government schemes research) are not anchored in class and should be flagged as optional extension or homework for interested students.

items.source_lo: Student identifies details, main ideas, and character motivations in a prose narrative by responding to comprehension, inference, and extract-analysis tasks.
items.item_stem:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi sits by the river weeping because she has never been allowed to go to school. The River, speaking in a quiet, personified voice, gives her a piece of advice. Read the passage below and answer the questions that follow.

'She had never been allowed to go to school — first she had to mind Ramu, then little Appu — while her brother Gopi and neighbour Meena went every morning. She was frightened it was too late. And yet she burned with curiosity: why were spiders yellow in yellow flowers? Why did bamboo trees rustle? Why did the moon always follow the same path?'

(a) What does Jahnavi's long list of 'why' questions reveal about her character?
(b) The River advises Jahnavi to 'slip into the school one morning and see what happens.' What quality does this advice encourage in Jahnavi?
(c) Jahnavi says, 'I'm so old now, they'll never let me go.' What can you infer about how she views her own situation from this line?

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: (a) Her questions reveal intense intellectual curiosity and a deep longing to understand the natural world around her, despite being denied formal schooling. (b) The River's advice encourages self-belief and courage — the willingness to take a risk and act despite fear. (c) The line reveals that Jahnavi has internalised the belief that she is too old and therefore unworthy of school, reflecting how social exclusion can erode a child's confidence.

items.question_text:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi sits by the river weeping because she has never been allowed to go to school. The River, speaking in a quiet, personified voice, gives her a piece of advice. Read the passage below and answer the questions that follow.

'She had never been allowed to go to school — first she had to mind Ramu, then little Appu — while her brother Gopi and neighbour Meena went every morning. She was frightened it was too late. And yet she burned with curiosity: why were spiders yellow in yellow flowers? Why did bamboo trees rustle? Why did the moon always follow the same path?'

(a) What does Jahnavi's long list of 'why' questions reveal about her character?
(b) The River advises Jahnavi to 'slip into the school one morning and see what happens.' What quality does this advice encourage in Jahnavi?
(c) Jahnavi says, 'I'm so old now, they'll never let me go.' What can you infer about how she views her own situation from this line?

items.source_lo: Student demonstrates listening comprehension by selecting words used in spoken passages.
items.item_stem: You will listen to five people speaking about school life. As you listen, circle the words in the box below that are actually used by the speakers. There are two words you will not need.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: Circle the words the five speakers actually use, verifying against the listening transcript (p.39). Two of the ten words are not used.

items.question_text: You will listen to five people speaking about school life. As you listen, circle the words in the box below that are actually used by the speakers. There are two words you will not need.

items.source_lo: Student participates in guided conversation to seek and provide advice, adapting tone and register to varied situational contexts.
items.item_stem:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi turns to the River when she faces a difficult situation and the River gives her clear, encouraging advice. Now imagine you are in the situation below. Work with a partner: one of you plays the person seeking advice, the other plays the adviser. Then swap roles.

Situation: A classmate from your school loves painting but has been told by a relative that art is not a serious subject and she should focus only on Mathematics and Science. She feels discouraged and is considering giving up art classes.

Seek and give advice using this structure:
— State the problem clearly.
— Ask for advice ('What do you think I should do?').
— Give at least two pieces of advice with reasons.
— Respond to the advice and explain your next step.

Speak naturally; do not read from a script.

expected_elements: States the problem clearly and seeks advice using a direct question.
Gives at least two pieces of advice, each supported by a brief reason.
Adapts tone — empathetic when seeking, encouraging when advising.
Concludes with a stated next step or decision, showing resolution.

items.question_text:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi turns to the River when she faces a difficult situation and the River gives her clear, encouraging advice. Now imagine you are in the situation below. Work with a partner: one of you plays the person seeking advice, the other plays the adviser. Then swap roles.

Situation: A classmate from your school loves painting but has been told by a relative that art is not a serious subject and she should focus only on Mathematics and Science. She feels discouraged and is considering giving up art classes.

Seek and give advice using this structure:
— State the problem clearly.
— Ask for advice ('What do you think I should do?').
— Give at least two pieces of advice with reasons.
— Respond to the advice and explain your next step.

Speak naturally; do not read from a script.

items.source_lo: Student writes a descriptive paragraph using sensory details and a structured prompt framework, giving a title and sustaining focus on observed natural phenomena.
items.item_stem:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi sits by the river and notices the world around her in rich detail — a kingfisher swooping, a lizard basking in the sun, the river murmuring softly. Nature is not just a backdrop; it is alive and full of meaning for her.

Now write a descriptive paragraph (100–120 words) about a natural scene you have observed — a river bank, a garden, a paddy field, a sky at dusk, or any place in nature that made an impression on you.

Your paragraph must:
— Have a title that reflects the scene.
— Open with a sentence that immediately draws the reader into the place.
— Include at least three sensory details (what you saw, heard, felt, smelled, or tasted).
— Use at least one comparison (simile or personification).
— End with a sentence that expresses how the scene made you feel or what it made you think.

expected_elements: Title is present and clearly connected to the described scene.
Opening sentence is vivid and draws the reader into the setting immediately.
At least three distinct sensory details are woven into the paragraph.
At least one simile or personification is used accurately and purposefully.
Closing sentence conveys a personal feeling or reflection, not a summary.

items.question_text:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi sits by the river and notices the world around her in rich detail — a kingfisher swooping, a lizard basking in the sun, the river murmuring softly. Nature is not just a backdrop; it is alive and full of meaning for her.

Now write a descriptive paragraph (100–120 words) about a natural scene you have observed — a river bank, a garden, a paddy field, a sky at dusk, or any place in nature that made an impression on you.

Your paragraph must:
— Have a title that reflects the scene.
— Open with a sentence that immediately draws the reader into the place.
— Include at least three sensory details (what you saw, heard, felt, smelled, or tasted).
— Use at least one comparison (simile or personification).
— End with a sentence that expresses how the scene made you feel or what it made you think.

items.source_lo: Student identifies and uses prepositions of place, direction, and time correctly, distinguishing them from adverbial uses of the same words in sentence context.
items.item_stem:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', the narrative is rich in words that show location and movement — Jahnavi crept into the back row, the River rushed toward the sea, Appu slept on her shoulder. Prepositions and adverbs that look identical (such as on, up, inside, through) behave differently depending on whether they are followed by a noun.

Read each sentence below. Write P if the word in parentheses is a preposition and A if it is an adverb. Then write one original sentence of your own using the same word in the opposite role (i.e., if the sentence uses it as a preposition, write your sentence using it as an adverb, and vice versa).

1. Jahnavi crept quietly through the classroom door. (target word: "through")
2. The kingfisher swooped down and then flew up again. (target words: "down", "up")
3. She placed sleeping Appu carefully on the bench beside her. (target word: "on")
4. The river rushed on through the mangrove roots without stopping. (target word: "on")
5. He stepped outside to call Jahnavi's father to the verandah. (target word: "outside")

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. P — 'through' links the verb 'crept' to the noun 'door'. Own sentence (adverb): 'She kept walking; the teacher's voice carried through.' 2. A — 'down' and 'up' modify the verb 'swooped'/'flew' with no following noun. Own sentence (preposition): 'The bird swooped down the steep bank.' 3. P — 'on' links 'placed' to 'bench'. Own sentence (adverb): 'She was tired but carried on.' 4. A — 'on' modifies 'rushed' with no following noun immediately. Own sentence (preposition): 'The catamaran sailed on the river at dawn.' 5. P — 'outside' links 'stepped' to an implied location with the following noun phrase. Own sentence (adverb): 'She waited nervously; he had gone outside.'

items.question_text:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', the narrative is rich in words that show location and movement — Jahnavi crept into the back row, the River rushed toward the sea, Appu slept on her shoulder. Prepositions and adverbs that look identical (such as on, up, inside, through) behave differently depending on whether they are followed by a noun.

Read each sentence below. Write P if the word in parentheses is a preposition and A if it is an adverb. Then write one original sentence of your own using the same word in the opposite role (i.e., if the sentence uses it as a preposition, write your sentence using it as an adverb, and vice versa).

1. Jahnavi crept quietly through the classroom door. (target word: "through")
2. The kingfisher swooped down and then flew up again. (target words: "down", "up")
3. She placed sleeping Appu carefully on the bench beside her. (target word: "on")
4. The river rushed on through the mangrove roots without stopping. (target word: "on")
5. He stepped outside to call Jahnavi's father to the verandah. (target word: "outside")

items.source_lo: Student analyses a visual text (poster) to identify its message about gender equity and connects it to broader social themes explored in the prose.
items.item_stem:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi's mother — who once longed for school herself but was denied by her own mother — warmly supports her daughter's wish to learn, saying that girls should learn as much as they want. The story ends with Jahnavi resolving to become a teacher who will bring all the village girls to school.

The poster below carries this message: 'The Happiness of a Nation lies in the Dignity of its Daughters.'

Answer the following questions:
(a) What connection can you make between Jahnavi's story and the poster's message? Use one detail from the prose to support your answer.
(b) The word 'dignity' means self-respect. In what way does being allowed to go to school give Jahnavi dignity?
(c) Do you agree that a nation's happiness depends on the dignity of its daughters? Give two reasons to support your view.

expected_elements: Connects the poster's message to Jahnavi's denied schooling using a specific story detail.
Explains dignity as self-worth restored by equal access to education.
Gives two reasoned arguments about girls' education and national well-being.
Response is structured: at least one paragraph per sub-question.

items.question_text:
In 'The Day the River Spoke', Jahnavi's mother — who once longed for school herself but was denied by her own mother — warmly supports her daughter's wish to learn, saying that girls should learn as much as they want. The story ends with Jahnavi resolving to become a teacher who will bring all the village girls to school.

The poster below carries this message: 'The Happiness of a Nation lies in the Dignity of its Daughters.'

Answer the following questions:
(a) What connection can you make between Jahnavi's story and the poster's message? Use one detail from the prose to support your answer.
(b) The word 'dignity' means self-respect. In what way does being allowed to go to school give Jahnavi dignity?
(c) Do you agree that a nation's happiness depends on the dignity of its daughters? Give two reasons to support your view.
